summary: This brief post shares a reader's account of Microsoft's internal source control practices during a 1997 internship. Ron Vogl describes how Microsoft used two competing tools: SourceSafe and an internal tool called Source Library Manager (SLM). Joel uses this anecdote to make a pointed observation: since Microsoft famously practices 'eating your own dog food,' the fact that many teams refused to use their own SourceSafe product speaks volumes about its quality.
key_points: Microsoft internally used both SourceSafe and an internal tool called SLM (Source Library Manager) in roughly equal amounts as of 1997
SLM was a command-line tool with a mix of SourceSafe and CVS-style workflows
Microsoft's own teams avoiding their own source control product is a damning signal about its quality, given their dogfooding culture
money_quotes: Given how devoted Microsoft is to eating your own dog food, the fact that there are still groups that don't use SourceSafe says something about how bad that product must be.
key_insight: When a company famous for dogfooding its own products has teams that refuse to use one of them, that's the strongest possible indictment of the product's quality.
